stem: A 3-year-old girl presents to her general practice with 36 hours of left ear pain and fever to 38.4. She has had two previous self-limiting episodes of similar pain. She is otherwise well, eating and drinking, and clinically not toxic. Otoscopy shows a bulging, immobile, opaque red left tympanic membrane without perforation, with normal contralateral findings. She lives in metropolitan Sydney and has no chronic conditions. She is not from an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander background.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 38.4
data_table.Examination: Left TM bulging, immobile, opaque, red; no perforation or discharge; right TM normal
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; no swabs or imaging

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Paracetamol 15 mg/kg every 4 hours plus oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days starting today
options.rationale: Routine immediate antibiotics are not first line in a non-toxic non-Indigenous child over 2 years with unilateral AOM and intact tympanic membrane; analgesia with 48-hour observation is appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Otitis media)

options.text: Analgesia alone with paracetamol or ibuprofen and parental review in 48 hours if there is no clinical improvement
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines support a watchful-waiting approach with analgesia in well non-Indigenous children over 2 with unilateral AOM and intact tympanic membrane, reserving antibiotics for failure to improve at 48 hours, perforation, or systemic illness. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Otitis media)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days and topical ciprofloxacin drops to the affected ear
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure after amoxicillin or perforation with discharge; first-line antibiotic when indicated is amoxicillin alone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Otitis media)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for 3 days with intranasal decongestants to reduce middle ear inflammation rapidly
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and decongestants have no demonstrated benefit in AOM and risk harm; analgesia is the cornerstone of supportive care. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Otitis media)

options.text: Urgent ENT review and tympanocentesis to obtain middle ear fluid for microbiology and targeted therapy choice
options.rationale: Tympanocentesis is not routine and is reserved for atypical, recurrent, or complicated cases not responding to standard care. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Otitis media)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media in well non-Indigenous Australian children over 2 years with unilateral disease and intact tympanic membrane is managed with analgesia and review at 48 hours. Antibiotics are reserved for children under 2, bilateral AOM, perforation with discharge, systemic illness, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander background, or failure to improve at 48 hours. When indicated the first-line agent is amoxicillin.
key_points: Watchful waiting suits non-toxic non-Indigenous unilateral AOM over 2
Amoxicillin first line when antibiotics indicated
Amoxicillin-clavulanate is second line for treatment failure or discharge
Antibiotics indicated in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children due to higher complication rates
explanation.pearls: Therapeutic Guidelines indications for immediate antibiotics in AOM: under 2 years, bilateral, perforation with discharge, systemic illness, immunocompromise, or Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander background.

stem: A 4-year-old boy presents on day 6 of fever above 39 with persistent fever despite paracetamol. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, a strawberry tongue with cracked red lips, polymorphous truncal rash, and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy 2 cm in diameter. He has erythematous palms with mild swelling. Streptococcal swab is negative and there is no contact with measles. Blood tests show neutrophilia, raised CRP, and thrombocytosis. He is reviewed in a paediatric inpatient unit. A baseline echocardiogram is being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 28, BP 96/58, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 39.4
data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection; red cracked lips; strawberry tongue; polymorphous rash; right cervical lymphadenopathy 2 cm; erythematous swollen palms
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4 x10^9/L (N 14.8), CRP 142 mg/L, platelets 612 x10^9/L; ASOT pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure and discharge with paracetamol; review only if fever lasts beyond day 14 of illness presentation
options.rationale: Delaying treatment beyond day 10 substantially increases coronary aneurysm risk; the priority is IVIG administration within the window. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin and oral roxithromycin for presumed group A streptococcal scarlet fever overlap
options.rationale: Scarlet fever does not produce conjunctivitis, mucositis, or palmar oedema; misdiagnosis delays IVIG and increases aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Oral aspirin 30 mg/kg/day in four divided doses without intravenous immunoglobulin and reassess at day 10
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient and does not reduce coronary aneurysm rates; IVIG is the disease-modifying treatment within the 10-day window. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 10 hours plus aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses
options.rationale: Five criteria of fever plus four of conjunctivitis, mucositis, rash, extremity changes, and cervical lymphadenopathy confirm Kawasaki disease; IVIG 2 g/kg within 10 days reduces coronary aneurysm rate from 25 percent to under 5 percent, with adjunct aspirin. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse alone with paracetamol around the clock for symptomatic relief
options.rationale: Adjunctive steroids are considered in high-risk or IVIG-resistant disease, not as monotherapy first line in straightforward Kawasaki within the IVIG window. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is diagnosed by fever lasting 5 days or more with at least 4 of bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral mucosal changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, and cervical lymphadenopathy at least 1.5 cm. IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion within 10 days of fever onset cuts coronary aneurysm risk from around 25 percent to under 5 percent. High-dose aspirin is added until afebrile then reduced to antiplatelet dose. Baseline and follow-up echocardiograms are mandatory.
key_points: 5 days fever + 4 of 5 features confirms diagnosis
IVIG 2 g/kg within 10 days dramatically reduces coronary aneurysm risk
Aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg/day initially then 3 to 5 mg/kg/day antiplatelet
Echocardiogram at baseline and follow-up 6 to 8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Kawasaki disease key threshold: IVIG 2 g/kg within day 10 of fever drops coronary aneurysm rate from 25 percent to under 5 percent; aspirin is adjunct, not substitute.

sources.label: AHA Scientific Statement: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Long-Term Management of Kawasaki Disease

stem: An 18-month-old previously well boy presents after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 18 minutes. The seizure terminated with intramuscular midazolam given by paramedics. He has a temperature of 39.1 and rhinorrhoea. He has fully regained his baseline neurological status over the past 2 hours. He has had one previous brief simple febrile seizure 4 months ago. Examination today shows no focal neurology, soft fontanelle, no neck stiffness, and no rash. He is feeding and interactive on the ward.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, RR 24, SpO2 99% room air, BP 92/56, Temp 39.1
data_table.Examination: Alert and interactive; soft fontanelle; no neck stiffness; no rash; no focal neurology; clear chest
data_table.Investigations: BSL 4.8 mmol/L; venous gas normal; FBC and UEC pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate further investigation pathway?

options.text: Urgent CT brain followed by lumbar puncture and electroencephalogram performed during the same admission today
options.rationale: Routine CT and EEG are not indicated in a fully recovered well child with prolonged febrile seizure and no focal neurology, raised ICP signs, or persistent altered consciousness. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

options.text: Identify and treat the febrile source, observe for several hours, and discharge with safety-net advice if returned to baseline
options.rationale: A complex febrile seizure in a child who has fully returned to baseline without focal signs requires investigation of the febrile source and observation only; brain imaging and EEG are not routinely required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

options.text: Lumbar puncture in all children under 24 months with any febrile seizure to exclude meningitis or encephalitis
options.rationale: Universal LP in children under 24 months is no longer supported; LP is indicated when meningitis is clinically suspected, when the child has not returned to baseline, or when pretreatment with antibiotics could mask features. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

options.text: Outpatient MRI brain plus electroencephalogram in 4 weeks and commence prophylactic sodium valproate today
options.rationale: Long-term anticonvulsant prophylaxis is not indicated for febrile seizures because the recurrence and subsequent epilepsy risk does not justify medication adverse effects. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

options.text: Admit to paediatric ICU for continuous EEG monitoring and 48 hours of intravenous antiepileptic loading therapy
options.rationale: ICU admission and EEG monitoring are not indicated after a self-terminating febrile seizure in a child who has returned to baseline; this overtreats a benign syndrome. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile seizures)

explanation.summary: Complex febrile seizures are those lasting more than 15 minutes, recurring within 24 hours, or focal. A well child who returns to baseline requires investigation of the febrile source and a period of observation, but routine imaging, EEG, and anticonvulsant prophylaxis are not indicated. LP and imaging are reserved for clinical suspicion of meningitis, altered consciousness, focal neurology, or signs of raised ICP. Recurrence risk after first febrile seizure is about 30 percent overall.
key_points: Complex = under 15 min, recurrent in 24 h, or focal
Investigate source of fever; do not routinely image
LP only if clinically suspicious for meningitis or not back to baseline
No role for anticonvulsant prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: Complex febrile seizure with full recovery and no focal signs: investigate the fever source, observe, and discharge with safety-netting. No routine CT, EEG, or anticonvulsants.

sources.label: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Febrile Seizures

subtopic_detail: Active management of third stage and PPH prevention
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 has just delivered a 3.8 kg baby vaginally at 39+5 weeks gestation in the birth suite after a 7 hour uncomplicated labour. The baby is on her chest and well. Her antenatal course was unremarkable, with no known coagulopathy and a baseline haemoglobin of 124 g/L. She has had one previous uncomplicated vaginal birth. She has no contraindications to oxytocic agents and no allergies. The placenta has not yet been delivered and there is minimal bleeding so far.

data_table.Examination: Uterus contracting; minimal blood loss approximately 100 mL so far; placenta undelivered
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (booking); coagulation screen normal; group and screen on file

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intervention for the third stage?

options.text: Wait for spontaneous placental delivery without uterotonic and clamp the cord after the placenta has emerged fully
options.rationale: Physiological third stage is associated with around 2 to 3 times the postpartum haemorrhage risk of active management; active management is recommended for all women without contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms given alone with subsequent controlled cord traction once contracting
options.rationale: Ergometrine monotherapy is not first line because hypertension is a contraindication and the side effect profile is inferior to oxytocin; combined oxytocin-ergometrine is reserved for higher-risk women without hypertension. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intramuscular oxytocin 10 units within one minute of birth followed by controlled cord traction once the uterus contracts
options.rationale: Active third stage management with intramuscular oxytocin 10 units, delayed cord clamping, and controlled cord traction reduces PPH risk and is recommended for all women without contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intravenous carboprost tromethamine 250 micrograms as routine prophylaxis with delayed cord clamping for 60 seconds
options.rationale: Carboprost is reserved for atonic PPH after oxytocin and ergometrine fail; its side effect burden makes it unsuitable for routine prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Sublingual misoprostol 600 micrograms alone with immediate cord clamping and assisted delivery of the placenta
options.rationale: Misoprostol is reserved for settings where injectable uterotonics are unavailable; oxytocin is more effective and is the agent of choice in an Australian birth suite. (Source: RANZCOG: Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Active management of the third stage of labour involves intramuscular oxytocin 10 units within 1 minute of birth, delayed cord clamping at 1 to 3 minutes when possible, and controlled cord traction with uterine guarding once the uterus contracts. This reduces postpartum haemorrhage rates compared with physiological management. Ergometrine combinations are added in higher-risk women without hypertension; carboprost and misoprostol are second-line agents.
key_points: Active management reduces PPH risk 2 to 3 fold
IM oxytocin 10 units within 1 minute is first line
Delayed cord clamping unless baby resuscitation required
Ergometrine contraindicated in hypertension
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG active third stage = IM oxytocin 10 units within 1 minute + delayed cord clamping + controlled cord traction; combination oxytocin-ergometrine reserved for higher-risk women without hypertension.

sources.label: WHO Recommendations for the Prevention and Treatment of PPH

stem: A 32-year-old G1 woman is found to have a twin pregnancy at her first booking visit, where her transvaginal ultrasound today demonstrates two live foetuses at 9+0 weeks gestation. She is otherwise well, IVF-conceived after a fresh single embryo transfer, and asks about her care plan. There are no foetal anomalies seen so far. She has no other medical conditions and is on standard antenatal vitamins. She is keen to optimise outcomes for her twins.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen consistent with early pregnancy
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal USS today: two viable intrauterine sacs at 9+0 weeks, single embryo transferred; chorionicity not yet documented

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in confirming chorionicity?

options.text: Schedule a repeat dating ultrasound between 11 and 13+6 weeks gestation to identify the lambda or T sign reliably
options.rationale: Accurate chorionicity determination is best between 11 and 13+6 weeks when the lambda sign indicates dichorionic and the T sign indicates monochorionic; after 14 weeks accuracy falls. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Send urgent chorionic villus sampling to determine zygosity and chorionicity together by genetic analysis today
options.rationale: Zygosity and chorionicity are not interchangeable; CVS carries a procedural loss risk and is not the routine approach to chorionicity determination, which is ultrasonographic. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Defer chorionicity assessment to the morphology scan at 19 to 20 weeks gestation when foetal anatomy is reviewed
options.rationale: Chorionicity becomes unreliable to determine by sign after 14 weeks; deferring to 20 weeks risks misclassification with implications for surveillance and outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Assume dichorionic-diamniotic from the IVF single embryo transfer history and proceed with usual antenatal care
options.rationale: A single embryo transfer that subsequently divides produces monochorionic twins; chorionicity must be confirmed by ultrasound rather than assumed from the conception history. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Determine chorionicity at the next ultrasound by counting placental masses alone without reference to inter-twin membrane
options.rationale: Two placental masses can occur in dichorionic and occasionally fused monochorionic twins; the lambda and T signs at the inter-twin membrane insertion are the accurate markers. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Chorionicity must be established as early as possible in twin pregnancy because it dictates surveillance frequency and risk of complications such as twin-to-twin transfusion. The first trimester between 11 and 13+6 weeks is the optimal window, using the lambda sign for dichorionic and T sign for monochorionic. Accuracy falls beyond 14 weeks and chorionicity cannot be reliably inferred from conception mode because single embryos can divide.
key_points: Best window 11 to 13+6 weeks for lambda vs T sign
Accuracy falls beyond 14 weeks
Single embryo transfer can still produce monochorionic twins
Counting placental masses alone is unreliable
explanation.pearls: Twin chorionicity is best determined at 11 to 13+6 weeks using lambda (dichorionic) vs T sign (monochorionic) at the inter-twin membrane insertion; do not infer from IVF embryo transfer history.

sources.label: NICE NG137: Twin and triplet pregnancy

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 at 31+4 weeks gestation is admitted with right upper quadrant pain, malaise, and a blood pressure of 158/104. Today's bloods show worsening haemolysis, transaminitis, and falling platelets compared with yesterday. She has received one dose of betamethasone yesterday and her second dose is due in 12 hours. The foetus is appropriately grown and the CTG is reassuring. Magnesium sulfate has been started for seizure prophylaxis. The intensive care and neonatology teams are on standby.

data_table.Examination: Tender right upper quadrant; hyperreflexia; abdomen soft; foetal heart 142 reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 68 x10^9/L (yesterday 102), ALT 220 U/L (yesterday 142), AST 232 U/L, LDH 720 U/L, haptoglobin low; Cr 92 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery decision?

options.text: Plan delivery within 24 to 48 hours after completion of the corticosteroid course assuming continued stability
options.rationale: HELLP with falling platelets and rising transaminases is progressive; delaying past 24 to 48 hours to complete steroids is acceptable only when biochemistry is stable, not deteriorating. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Defer delivery until 34 weeks given foetal wellbeing reassuring; treat with high-dose corticosteroid pulses for HELLP
options.rationale: Expectant management to 34 weeks in HELLP with progressive maternal deterioration risks abruption, eclampsia, hepatic rupture, and DIC; high-dose steroids do not modify maternal HELLP progression. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Deliver by category 2 caesarean section now without waiting for the second corticosteroid dose to be administered
options.rationale: Worsening biochemistry with falling platelets, rising transaminases, and persistent symptoms in HELLP mandates delivery without delay; partial corticosteroid benefit is accepted and category 2 caesarean is appropriate given the cervix and prematurity. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Commence prostaglandin induction of labour and aim for vaginal birth over the next 12 to 24 hours of admission
options.rationale: Induction with progressing biochemistry and prematurity is unlikely to achieve timely birth before maternal complications; expedited caesarean is preferred. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Transfuse platelets prophylactically to a target of 100 x10^9/L and continue conservative inpatient management
options.rationale: Prophylactic platelets do not stop the HELLP progression and routine transfusion threshold for delivery is around 50 for caesarean; this does not address the underlying need for delivery. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome with progressive biochemistry (falling platelets, rising transaminases) at 31 to 34 weeks gestation mandates delivery without waiting to complete antenatal corticosteroids. Mode of delivery balances cervical status, gestation, and maternal stability; emergent caesarean is common at very preterm gestations or when induction is unlikely to succeed quickly. Magnesium sulfate is given for seizure prophylaxis, blood pressure is controlled, and corrective transfusion is reserved for active bleeding or operative thresholds.
key_points: Progressive HELLP biochemistry mandates delivery
Do not wait for full steroid course if deteriorating
Caesarean common at very preterm gestation
Platelet target for caesarean around 50 x10^9/L; do not transfuse prophylactically
explanation.pearls: HELLP delivery threshold: progressive worsening (falling platelets, rising AST/ALT) means deliver now; finishing the corticosteroid course is desirable but does not override deterioration.

subtopic_detail: Community-acquired pneumonia CURB-65 site of care
stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 3 days of productive cough, fevers, and dyspnoea. He has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He lives alone and is independent at baseline. On examination he is alert with focal right basal crepitations, oxygen saturation 91 percent on room air, and a respiratory rate of 32. Chest x-ray confirms right lower lobe consolidation. He has not previously had pneumonia and has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Examination: Right basal crackles; no signs of consolidation outside; alert; mildly confused (AMTS 8/10)
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, CRP 162, urea 9.4 mmol/L, Cr 110 micromol/L, BSL 12.4 mmol/L; CXR: right lower lobe consolidation; lactate 1.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate site of care and empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Discharge with oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily for 5 days and general practitioner review in 48 hours
options.rationale: CURB-65 here is at least 4 (confusion, urea over 7, RR at least 30, BP under 90/60-borderline, age 65+), placing the patient at high risk and mandating inpatient management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP)

options.text: Admit to short-stay unit on oral amoxicillin-clavulanate with planned discharge within 24 hours of presentation
options.rationale: Short-stay with oral therapy is suited to low-to-moderate severity disease; this patient's vitals and biochemistry place him in moderate-to-severe CAP requiring intravenous therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP)

options.text: Admit to general medical ward on intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Moderate-severity CAP in an Australian inpatient is treated with IV benzylpenicillin plus an atypical-cover agent such as doxycycline; SMART-COP can refine ICU referral but ward admission is appropriate when oxygen and perfusion are correctable. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP)

options.text: Admit to intensive care unit with intravenous meropenem and intravenous vancomycin to cover all resistant organisms
options.rationale: Empiric meropenem and vancomycin are reserved for severe CAP with risk factors for resistant pathogens or in immunocompromise; this presentation lacks those features. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP)

options.text: Admit to general medical ward on intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus oral azithromycin 500 mg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved for moderate-severe CAP with intolerance or local resistance concerns; the Australian first-line in moderate disease is IV benzylpenicillin plus an oral atypical agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - CAP)

explanation.summary: Australian CAP management uses CURB-65 (or SMART-COP) to direct site of care. Score 0 to 1 typically outpatient with oral amoxicillin; 2 inpatient on oral or IV therapy; 3 or more inpatient with IV therapy and consider ICU. Moderate CAP empiric therapy is IV benzylpenicillin with an oral atypical agent (doxycycline preferred over macrolide in Australia for stewardship). Severe disease or septic shock prompts ceftriaxone plus azithromycin and ICU.
key_points: CURB-65 of 3 or more = inpatient IV therapy
AU first line moderate CAP: IV benzylpenicillin + doxycycline
SMART-COP refines ICU referral
Doxycycline preferred over azithromycin for atypical cover in AU
explanation.pearls: CURB-65 in this case: confusion + urea over 7 + RR over 30 + age 65+ = 4 - inpatient IV therapy mandated; AU first line is benzylpenicillin + doxycycline.

sources.label: TSANZ Position Statement: Community-acquired pneumonia in adults

stem: A 64-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with 6 weeks of progressive nasal crusting, recurrent epistaxis, hearing loss, and intermittent haemoptysis. Over the past week she has noticed dark urine. Her bloods are remarkable for raised inflammatory markers and a rising creatinine. Urinalysis shows red cell casts and proteinuria. She is afebrile, alert, and her chest x-ray shows multiple non-cavitating peripheral nodules. ANCA testing has been requested. She is referred urgently to medical assessment.

data_table.Examination: Crusting nasal septum; saddle-nose deformity early; conductive hearing loss; no focal neurology; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Cr 168 micromol/L (baseline 78); urine PCR 280 mg/mmol, RBCs and casts; CRP 92 mg/L; ANCA pending; CXR with multiple non-cavitating peripheral nodules

lead_in: What is the most likely ANCA pattern in this presentation?

options.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibody (anti-GBM) positivity without ANCA positivity in the typical case
options.rationale: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture) causes pulmonary-renal syndrome but is not associated with sinonasal or otological involvement and typically lacks the granulomatous upper airway disease seen here. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021)

options.text: Cytoplasmic ANCA pattern targeting proteinase-3 (PR3-ANCA), classically associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis
options.rationale: Upper respiratory tract granulomatous involvement (sinusitis, saddle nose, hearing loss) with renal and pulmonary disease is classic for granulomatosis with polyangiitis, which most commonly shows cytoplasmic ANCA staining with PR3 specificity. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021)

options.text: Perinuclear ANCA pattern targeting myeloperoxidase, classically associated with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
options.rationale: EGPA (Churg-Strauss) typically features asthma, blood eosinophilia, and migratory pulmonary infiltrates with peripheral neuropathy; upper airway destructive disease is not its signature. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021)

options.text: Atypical ANCA pattern with no recognised target antigen, classically associated with secondary drug-induced vasculitis
options.rationale: Drug-induced ANCA vasculitis is typically MPO-ANCA, and the granulomatous upper airway picture is not characteristic of these atypical patterns. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021)

options.text: Negative ANCA testing with positive cryoglobulins and reduced complement levels, classically suggesting cryoglobulinaemia
options.rationale: Cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis typically presents with palpable purpura, peripheral neuropathy, and low complement, often with hepatitis C; the destructive upper airway picture is not characteristic. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2021)

explanation.summary: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (formerly Wegener) classically presents with granulomatous involvement of the upper airway (chronic sinusitis, nasal crusting, saddle nose, hearing loss), lower airway (cavitating or non-cavitating nodules), and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with red cell casts and proteinuria. Cytoplasmic ANCA targeting PR3 is the typical pattern. Microscopic polyangiitis is more often pauci-immune renal disease with MPO-ANCA. EGPA features asthma, eosinophilia, and neuropathy.
key_points: GPA: upper airway destruction + pulmonary nodules + RPGN
GPA typically c-ANCA targeting PR3
MPA typically p-ANCA targeting MPO; pauci-immune renal
EGPA: asthma + eosinophilia + neuropathy; often MPO-ANCA
explanation.pearls: Pattern recognition for GPA: saddle nose + sensorineural or conductive hearing loss + cavitating or non-cavitating lung nodules + RPGN with red cell casts = c-ANCA/PR3.

sources.label: EULAR/ERA-EDTA recommendations for the management of ANCA-associated vasculitis

stem: A 32-year-old man on day 14 of carbamazepine for newly diagnosed epilepsy presents with 24 hours of malaise, fever, and a painful pink macular rash on his torso. By the time he reaches the emergency department, he has developed early painful blistering of the oral mucosa, conjunctival hyperaemia, and Nikolsky-positive areas covering an estimated 12 percent of his body surface area. He is haemodynamically stable but visibly uncomfortable. His other medications include paracetamol PRN and a probiotic. There is no preceding upper respiratory infection.

data_table.Examination: Pink macular rash on trunk and limbs; Nikolsky positive on shoulder; lip and oral mucosal erosions; bilateral conjunctival injection
data_table.Investigations: Hb 138 g/L, WCC 11.2 x10^9/L, CRP 64 mg/L, Cr 84 micromol/L, ALT 92 U/L; urea 5.2; bicarbonate 22

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Cease the carbamazepine and arrange urgent transfer to a tertiary burns unit with multidisciplinary support
options.rationale: SJS/TEN is a dermatological emergency; the culprit drug must be ceased and patients should be transferred to a burns unit for fluid balance, wound care, ophthalmology, urology and pain support, especially with epidermal detachment over 10 percent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology - SCAR)

options.text: Continue carbamazepine because of seizure risk and treat the rash with potent topical corticosteroids and emollients
options.rationale: The culprit drug must be stopped immediately; continuing it risks progression to TEN with much higher mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology - SCAR)

options.text: Start empirical intravenous flucloxacillin for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome until cultures available
options.rationale: SSSS occurs predominantly in children and lacks mucosal involvement; mucosal involvement with a drug exposure makes SJS/TEN the working diagnosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology - SCAR)

options.text: Admit under dermatology to the general ward and commence high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for skin involvement
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids have mixed evidence in SJS/TEN and are not first-line; burns-unit-level supportive care, often with cyclosporine or IVIG depending on local protocol, is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology - SCAR)

options.text: Wait 48 hours to see whether erythema multiforme resolves spontaneously before triggering specialty referral pathways
options.rationale: Erythema multiforme is target lesions on extremities without significant epidermal detachment; Nikolsky positivity and 12 percent BSA detachment are SJS-TEN overlap and require urgent escalation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology - SCAR)

explanation.summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis sit on a spectrum defined by epidermal detachment: SJS under 10 percent BSA, overlap 10 to 30, TEN over 30. Carbamazepine is a major culprit and may have HLA-B*15:02 association in some populations. Immediate steps are to cease the culprit drug, transfer to a burns unit, and provide multidisciplinary supportive care including fluids, eye care, urinary, and pain. Mortality rises sharply with later cessation of the culprit drug.
key_points: SJS under 10 percent BSA; overlap 10 to 30; TEN over 30
Cease culprit drug immediately and transfer to burns unit
Carbamazepine is a high-risk culprit
Systemic steroids are not first line; supportive care primary
explanation.pearls: SJS/TEN: cease culprit drug (often allopurinol, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, sulfa, NSAID) and transfer to burns unit; SCORTEN within 24 h predicts mortality.

sources.label: Australasian College of Dermatologists position on SJS/TEN

stem: A 52-year-old man with a 30 pack-year smoking history attends his general practice motivated to quit. He has previously attempted nicotine patches alone twice without success and finds withdrawal craving the major barrier. He has stable coronary artery disease, no history of seizures, and no current depression or psychiatric symptoms. He sleeps reasonably and has no nightmares. He is open to combination pharmacotherapy and is supported by his partner. He is keen to know the most effective evidence-based option.

data_table.Examination: No acute respiratory distress; clear chest; no neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods unremarkable; spirometry FEV1/FVC 75 percent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy option?

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily monotherapy escalating to twice daily after 7 days of treatment for 12 weeks total
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but its abstinence rates are lower than varenicline and its seizure risk excludes it in some patients; varenicline is preferred when not contraindicated. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily titrated to 1 mg twice daily over 1 week, started 1 week before nominated quit date
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation; the standard schedule is titrate from 0.5 mg daily up to 1 mg twice daily started 1 week before quit day, with treatment to 12 weeks (or 24 in some). (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Nicotine replacement therapy patches alone at the same strength previously tried with planned 8-week reducing regimen
options.rationale: The patient has already failed patch monotherapy twice; repeating the same approach without escalation is unlikely to succeed and combination NRT or varenicline is more effective. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Brief advice and follow-up review in 3 months because all pharmacotherapy options carry significant adverse event risks
options.rationale: Brief advice alone is less effective than combined behavioural and pharmacological support; pharmacotherapy in this motivated patient is appropriate and standard. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Electronic cigarette substitution with nicotine vaporiser as a less harmful alternative under prescription
options.rationale: Nicotine vaporisers are available on prescription in Australia for cessation when first-line options fail or are unsuitable; they are not the first-line option for a motivated patient with no contraindication to varenicline. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian smoking cessation guidance places varenicline as the most effective single agent with continuous abstinence rates at 12 weeks roughly double that of placebo and superior to NRT or bupropion monotherapy. Combination NRT (patch plus oral) is an alternative when varenicline is contraindicated. Behavioural support such as Quitline counselling improves outcomes. Nicotine vaporisers are second line in Australia.
key_points: Varenicline is first-line single agent
Combination NRT (patch + oral) is alternative
Behavioural support always added
Vaporisers on prescription are second line in AU
explanation.pearls: Varenicline regimen: start 0.5 mg daily 1 week before quit day, titrate to 1 mg BD by day 8, continue 12 weeks; double abstinence rate over placebo; mental health symptoms now NOT a contraindication per updated PBS criteria.
